item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spec-tacular. +1

The anomaly is clear, there's a character arc, there's foundation backstory that's specific enough to bite hard but ambiguous enough to be conceptualized within general headcanon.

There's even the spice of nightmare fuel

To My Successor

but in regards to your questions around familial and romantic ties

OVERSEER COUNCIL ACTION #4057-2

RESULT: MOTION PASSES

Oofdah. Gut punch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just beautiful. Anyways +1. Sorry for the necrovote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this - it's lovingly written and the implications for the rest of the O5 council are really interesting. I agree with previous commenters that the final letter should be hidden in some way - I also read it after tab 1 and spoiled myself. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's articles like this that remind me why I love this site. I wish I could upvote more than once but sadly, I can't so I guess +1 will have to do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A weird nitpick here, but: we didn't have the word "meme", let alone "memetic", until the 1976 publication of The Selfish Gene.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A well written O5 piece is always nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the past few weeks i have been reading the series 3 and 4 (for old time's sake), and missed the competition completely. Sorry that i couldn't give you support during the competition, but hell, this is so damn well done.

+1 heartily, and hope this reach 200 soon!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really lovely work for your first article and what an interesting and emotional tie in to the history of the council. I agree with some others though, the scale of the fourth incident is a bit too much for me. Having them request troops from every country and that being what clues the council in rather than actually receiving them or something similar would work better in my opinion. Otherwise the scale just seems too huge even with the timeframe you set up.

Still an upvote from me though! This was a very compelling article. I am the mother of a young child and hoo boy did this resonate with me. If you had much of the foundation's resources at your disposal, what wouldn't you do to protect your family? And what a heartbreaking result

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same. I would hide it or such because I read it after the first tab so confused/spoiled myself. Lovely article though, especially for your first one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this a lot.

This has nothing to do with the quality, but I also really liked that it keeps up the strange trend of making Two and Seven always women in the Foundation's history (even in, say, Resurrection, which I pick solely due to being Resurrection's creator/editor, there are other women on the Council, but Two and Seven are the long-term members, not counting One's possible alternates). Has nothing to do with my upvote, just an odd little quirk that I enjoyed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would be like Arby's employees talking to each other in hushed tones about how important it is that They Have The Meats. No matter how true or important, it would sound silly in any workforce after a while.

This is a venture worth exploring.

Just to not be off topic, I love this article. It has a certain clarity while still retaining the horror of something clearly going wrong. The second to last one especially, is great when you realize what is actually happening.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have 2 big problems with this. The escalation to all governments giving over 10,000 soldiers to this thing wouldn't happen over night, which means it would be a pattern of observable weird behaviour that would be noted by the O5 council much sooner.

But, more importantly, how would O5-7 not immediately notice something was wrong the minute her daughter handed herself into the Foundation for containment because people were following her around, and then people keep showing up out of nowhere to see her?

I just don't buy that they would be quiet about it for the 5 years this took to escalate while her daughter is stuck in a containment cell by herself for that entire time.

I also have a lot of the same issues with tab 4 that a lot of other people have mentioned, especially with the footnotes.

Some issues I noted.
– hide block

It is unknown how SCP-4000 was aware of the location of site 19, and has not revealed such information when questioned.

Capital S on Site 19

SCP-4000 is to be contained in a hermetically sealed special containment cell in site 19.

And again.

Multiple members of the O5 council are currently under investigation.

Under investigation for what? This line seems to come out of nowhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This goes against my headcanon in too many ways for me to appreciate the story. O5-7 does all that and merely gets demoted and amnesticized? MC&D is better at memetics than the Foundation? New O5s can't have any living family members? I gotta downvote.

Also, the formatting of the O5 notes in the tabs is screwed up on mobile (goes beyond the tab borders).

item.title: Re: +50!
item.content:encoded: It's a really great article, you should be proud of yourself! It flowed really well, told an interesting story, and got me a little emotional toward the end. Please keep writing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it’s beautiful. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Came here to say the same. I'd suggest to hide that info. This article is brilliant, maybe a picture will help too.

+1 and hats off for being the first article!

item.title: I love it!
item.content:encoded:
The awkward thing about the SCP-4000 is that being about history it's hard to differentiate it from an SCP-001 proposal. A lot of SCP-4000's seemed to still get around this while still fitting the theme though which is very impressive. And the only thing that I can see about this article that doesn't make it seem like an SCP-001 is that it is fit inside a different context (I.e certain groups already existing, the U.N, different year). Also the E.U (As mentioned in the article) didn't exist during the time this article was set in. Nevertheless these are nitpicks and I still think that your SCP-4000 article is the best I've seen so far.
So I love it and I need to make it clear in my title but I must make my nitpicks where they're due.

I'm up-voting and i'm getting all my friends to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done on your first article my dude. It takes guts to post to the wiki for the first time, even more so during a major contest.

I greatly enjoyed this article. I like the changes to the document (thought the Save Her bit in the fourth iteration goes a little over the top, imo) over time, as well as the approach that this is not the actual 4000 but rather an object from history kept on file as an example to explain one of the caveats of being an O5. We have a lot of objects that explore the history of the Foundation, but this one really stands out to me as a reader.

+1. Well done, and welcome to the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a first article, this is incredibly impressive. I look forward to reading your future works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my opinion the buildup to the climax is a little too quick. However I love the concept of the article, and the execution is otherwise excellent. +1

item.title: Going away...
item.content:encoded: I'm going to be away for 2 weeks, which means I'll be confined to mobile. I won't be able to make changes to the article for now, but I'll still be able to spill the tea here in the discussion section. Anyhoo, see you guys soonish!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this one, as it very quickly and efficiently meets the contest requirements and is interesting as all hell. I also think it could be a lot more in depth and use some general… I dunno. Development? Baking? How were the council members immune off the bat? What kind of events would have happened as the meme spread and people got more and more insane about their devotion to the idea? Did -7 spend some time arguing with the Council on what to do rather than (as the text makes it feel) just go straight to essentially treason?

item.title: Re: +50!
item.content:encoded:
It seems there's quite a few of us longtime lurkers coming out to submit for the first time in 4000, and none of us expect to do well.

Don't start celebrating yet- I'd be surprised if you don't hit +100. This little skip of your has heart.

I'm cheering for you.

EDIT: +100. Called it! Congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not what the quote said.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: your family dont have to be dead they just kind of erase you out of the lives of everybody you care about w amnestics

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is a hell of a first effort. good work, thanks for writing it
I don't even know what to say at this point, just thanks to everyone who's upvoted so far, and even the ones who have downvoted, your honesty is really appreciated!

item.title: +25 and then some!
item.content:encoded: I woke up to find my article at +47 SOMEHOW?! Umm… Thank you all! A LOT! IT'S GREAT! I only expected to get +25 over the course of the entire competition but somehow here we are! I dunno I guess I'll go brag on site19 or something stupid like that…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a fan of these kind stories. 'secure contain protect' is also a bad motto in almost every context, it's declaration of the foundation propose, it doesnt really work for stating loyalty or even as a general motto.

Also:

MOTION AS FOLLOWS: ENSURE ALL CHOSEN MEMBERS OF OVERSEER COUNCIL HAVE NO LIVING IMMEDIATE FAMILY PRIOR TO APPOINTMENT AS OVERSEER.

Breaks my suspension of disbelief. It's not something any organization would do after this type of event, it pretty much means no one can join the O5 now, and the issue wasn't living relatives it was breaking protocols.

My main issue though is that too much of this relies on emotional connection from the reader. When you don't have that, many things stick out like a sore thumb. It feels like the emotion leads the narrative rather then the other way around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't really like the 4th tab. It seems like an attempt to emulate other articles that use bizarre, stream-of-consciousness writing, but it doesn't fit with the rest of the article or contribute much to the overall story.

Other than that, I thought this was pretty solid and would consider upvoting in other circumstances, but as a contest entry the link to history is a little too tenuous for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Regarding tab 4, I like the SCP portion of it, with the nations sending more and more soldiers to protect her… I think the description is weak. You don't need to go full "descent into madness," you can write it coherently and just say things like, "All nations of the world agree that the safeguarding of SCP-4000 is of paramount importance."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it enough that I upvoted it, but I feel like the screwy nature of the 4th tab could be executed in a different way. I'm not exactly sure HOW, but it feels like it's not quite there. But the overall story really was nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a good contest entry, but definitely a good article. I have a few thoughts, however.

First, the formatting is confusing, in two ways. The 'folder tabs' don't change appreciably when clicked, so it's hard to know which one you're on. (The tab itself moves up slightly, but as the text does not, I expect this is a coding mistake rather than an intended feature.) Second, with the second part of the email or whatever at the bottom, I assumed it was a continuation of the article and read it after reading the first folder tab, which made no sense at the time. I'm not sure how better you could position it.

I also feel like the footnotes in part four are 'narmy', I guess is the word. I don't even know what they're supposed to be. The depiction of the Foundation succumbing to this meme is pretty great — if nothing else, I really like the object itself and the memetic effect — but it maybe goes a little overboard. Mostly, like I said, I don't know what those footnotes are meant to represent, because usually footnotes are just that, not snatches of things overheard as an article is written.

Upvote after contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the directive about the O5 position now requiring to be without any relatives is one of the most emotionally resonating part of the article, because it is one of those things that you kind of already expected to be true but to have it put in such a direct and cold way, directly in contrast with the warm affection that -7 had for her daughter makes it a chilling reminder of the darkest side of working for an organization like the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpick: the final text block starting with "Love makes us do the most irrational things" should be inside the final tab because it kind of spoils the rest of the article if you accidentally look at it.

Otherwise +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just as a suggestion - maybe delete the fourth tab, but move your researcher's ramble in to the third tap description? I can't help but agree with the concerns on the fourth tab, which also loses the pace of the article.

Or… change fourth tab to an Internal Investigation Report, an explanation of how they found out what the O5 did to cause all this?

Just ideas. But a very sweet, sad piece that I enjoyed.

item.title: Re: Hoo boy...
item.content:encoded:
I really like this 4000con entry, because it's not only interesting but manages to make me feel for the characters, all without resorting to some whacky gimmick like Doctor Spanko saving the Earth from an invasion of extradimensional aliens by rigging the 2000 election against a semi-possessed/paranormally empowered Al Gore (which in all fairness I did upvote).

This was good work Blasko, and I look forward to seeing future works from you.

item.title: Re: +10!
item.content:encoded: Grats.

And only in a matter of hours! Thank you guys so much, this means a lot!

Yes I'm going to one of these for each milestone, I wanna show my appreciation dammit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It made me feel sad for the O5 Council.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perfectly understandable, I've noticed the 4th tab is very divisive. As for the O5s saying "Secure, Contain, Protect", I've only used it at the end of the letters, as a call sign of sorts. As I recall, a lot of skips that have formal letters from O5s always end the documents with those words, which is why I did it.

Concerning the 4th tab and the O5s reacting, if you look at the time period between the 4th and 5th iteration, the O5 council reacted within one month of things going south. The dates of each iteratuion are there for a reason.

Anyway, I can understand your choice to novote, and sorry if I sounded snarky just then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like this, were it not for the fact that 3999 did the "article descends into insanity" trope first and, in my opinion, did it better. It's not a bad article, I just don't like it. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a few things that bug me here. (I remember reading this in chat, but I don't remember if I gave you any critique.) Most prominently:

1. "Secure, Contain, Protect" as a talisman that Foundation operatives just…drop casually in conversation all the time. It's common, but I always find it trite. It would be like Arby's employees talking to each other in hushed tones about how important it is that They Have The Meats. No matter how true or important, it would sound silly in any workforce after a while.

2. The containment procedures venture into insanity by the fourth tab. Which would be understandable if this ended in the end of the world as we know it, but it ends in the rest of the Council saying "hey, what say we undo all this?" At which point you wonder why they didn't figure to do that before all the nations of the world contributed, like, brigade-sized forces to some "inconspicuous" spot in the world to protect something they weren't allowed to know existed.

I don't dislike it. It has the right scale for the 4000 contest, and I like the sweetness of the overall story, but too much of it rubs me the wrong way. No-vote for now.

item.title: Re: Voting Module
item.content:encoded: Nah dude, it's good, just wanted to remind you, it's a really cool article, could have been part of the Doomsday contest if tweaked a bit so things weren't set right, +1 from me, when the module goes up that is lol.

item.title: Re: Voting Module
item.content:encoded: Sorry, I'm a little anxious, I've probbably missed a TON of things.

item.title: Hoo boy...
item.content:encoded:
Welp, I have sealed my fate. At this point I either die or get killed. For the record this is my first article, and I don't expect it to get anywhere close to winning. Anyway, the list of accolades is long but includes (I'm using site 19 usernames here, if it doesn't match your mainsite username tell me and I'll fix it):

CaptainKirby for some really nice in depth crit, and motivation
DogTeeth for helping me name Marie
Incidental_Cabbage for giving me the motivation to post this bad boy
DrChandra, Weryllium, Rounderhouse, SurrealRose, Lazar and SeptemberJackal for general crit, as well as the entire cast of site19 for putting up with my relentless search for crit.

Well, all that's left to do is sit back and hug something because I genuinely feel light headed now…

item.title: Voting Module
item.content:encoded: I believe this article needs one, unless I'm missing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nice
